title: Share of all cancer deaths by risk factor
subtitle: Estimated annual share of all [cancer](#dod:cancer) deaths attributed to each [risk factor](#dod:risk-factor).
note: To allow for comparisons between countries and over time, this metric is [age-standardized](#dod:age_standardized). Multiple risk factors can contribute to the same deaths; therefore, deaths from different risk factors cannot be summed up.
caption:
Share of all cancer deaths by risk factor

Estimated annual share of all cancer deaths attributed to each risk factor.

This horizontal bar chart displays the estimated annual share of all cancer deaths attributed to specific risk factors in the United States for the year 2023. The data is age-standardized to allow for comparisons between countries and over time. Tobacco is the leading risk factor, accounting for 21.2% of all cancer deaths, a figure that is nearly three times higher than the next category. Dietary risks follow at 7.2%, closely trailed by High BMI at 6.9% and High fasting plasma glucose at 6.3%. Occupational risks account for 4.8% of the share, while High alcohol use represents 3.6%. The remaining factors fall below 2%, with Drug use at 1.5%, Unsafe sex at 1.2%, and Environmental risks at 1%. Low physical activity and Air pollution occupy the bottom of the chart with 0.9% and 0.7% respectively. The note accompanying the chart clarifies that multiple risk factors can contribute to the same deaths; therefore, the percentages for different risk factors cannot be summed to reach a total. The visual arrangement highlights a significant gap between the impact of tobacco and all other listed behavioral and environmental contributors.
